module.title: Week 01: IT Fundamentals & Computer Hardware
module.output: Build your first home lab VM

topics.title: Day 1: How Computers Work
topics.description: Understand CPU, RAM, storage, and the fetch-execute cycle
topics.content:
## Day 1: How Computers Work

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Hardware knowledge is a must-have for every SOC analyst. When you triage alerts, you need to know what a CPU is, how memory works, and what happens during boot. Entry-level interviews test this. Without hardware fundamentals, you will not understand exploits or forensic artifacts.

:::objectives
- Understand CPU
- RAM
- Storage
- Fetch-execute cycle
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Explain the difference between RAM and storage to a beginner in cybersecurity. Use analogies.
topics.interviewQuestion: What is the difference between volatile and non-volatile memory?
topics.interviewAnswer: Volatile memory (RAM) loses data when power is off. Non-volatile (SSD/HDD) retains data. In forensics, you capture RAM first (order of volatility) before powering down.

quiz.question: What does CPU stand for?
quiz.explanation: CPU stands for Central Processing Unit - the brain of the computer that executes instructions.

quiz.question: Which memory type is volatile?
quiz.explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile - all data is lost when the power is turned off. SSDs and HDDs are non-volatile storage.

quiz.question: How many bits in a byte?
quiz.explanation: 1 byte = 8 bits. This is the standard unit for measuring data storage and memory.

quiz.question: What 2018 CPU vulnerability read kernel memory?
quiz.explanation: Meltdown (2018) exploited CPU speculative execution to read kernel memory. It affected Intel processors and required OS-level patches.

quiz.question: 1024 GB equals what?
quiz.explanation: 1 Terabyte (TB) = 1024 Gigabytes (GB). This is the standard progression in digital storage.

quiz.question: What is the primary function of RAM?
quiz.explanation: RAM temporarily holds data the CPU needs right now. It is much faster than storage but loses data when power is off.

quiz.question: Which component executes instructions?
quiz.explanation: The CPU fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, and executes them billions of times per second.

quiz.question: What does GHz measure in a CPU?
quiz.explanation: Gigahertz (GHz) measures clock speed - how many billions of cycles per second the CPU can execute.

quiz.question: Which storage type is typically faster?
quiz.explanation: SSDs (Solid State Drives) are significantly faster than HDDs because they use flash memory with no moving parts.

quiz.question: What is the fetch-execute cycle?
quiz.explanation: The fetch-execute cycle is how a CPU works: fetch an instruction from RAM, decode it, execute it, then repeat billions of times per second.

quiz.question: What is cache memory?
quiz.explanation: CPU cache is extremely fast memory built into the processor that stores frequently accessed data. L1 is fastest and smallest, L3 is slower but larger.

quiz.question: Which vulnerability exploits branch prediction?
quiz.explanation: Spectre exploits branch prediction (how CPUs guess future instructions) to leak data from other processes. It affects nearly all modern CPUs.

quiz.question: What is endianness?
quiz.explanation: Endianness defines whether the most significant byte (big-endian) or least significant byte (little-endian) comes first in memory. x86 CPUs use little-endian.

quiz.question: How does ASLR relate to CPU architecture?
quiz.explanation: Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR) randomizes where code loads in memory, making buffer overflow exploitation much harder. It relies on CPU memory management features.

quiz.question: What happens during a buffer overflow at the hardware level?
quiz.explanation: A buffer overflow writes data beyond a buffer's boundary, overwriting adjacent memory. At the hardware level, this corrupts data in RAM that the CPU then executes, potentially leading to code execution.

topics.title: Day 2: Operating Systems Overview
topics.description: Compare Windows, Linux, and macOS from a security perspective
topics.content:
## Day 2: Operating Systems Overview

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** OS knowledge is tested in every single cybersecurity interview. You will be asked about Windows vs Linux. Employers expect fluency in at least one command-line OS (Linux) and familiarity with Windows. SOC roles require both.

:::objectives
- Compare Windows
- Linux
- MacOS from a security perspective
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Create a comparison table of Windows vs Linux for a cybersecurity student. Include common tools, file systems, and security features.
topics.interviewQuestion: What is the kernel and why is it a security target?
topics.interviewAnswer: The kernel is the core OS component managing processes, memory, and system calls. Kernel exploits give attackers complete system control, bypassing all security controls. This is why kernel hardening is critical.

quiz.question: Which OS dominates enterprise servers?
quiz.explanation: Linux dominates enterprise servers with over 70% market share. Most cloud infrastructure runs on Linux.

quiz.question: What is the kernel?
quiz.explanation: The kernel is the core of any operating system. It manages processes, memory, devices, and system calls. Compromising the kernel gives attackers complete control.

quiz.question: Which distro is built for pentesting?
quiz.explanation: Kali Linux is specifically designed for penetration testing and comes pre-installed with hundreds of security tools.

quiz.question: What is Active Directory?
quiz.explanation: Active Directory is Microsoft's identity management service for Windows domain networks. It manages users, computers, and permissions centrally.

quiz.question: What is SELinux?
quiz.explanation: SELinux (Security-Enhanced Linux) is a mandatory access control (MAC) security module in the Linux kernel. It enforces security policies beyond standard permissions.

quiz.question: Which file system does Windows use?
quiz.explanation: NTFS (New Technology File System) is the primary file system for Windows. It supports permissions, encryption (EFS), and journaling.

quiz.question: What is a system call (syscall)?
options: A request to the kernel from a program
quiz.explanation: System calls are how programs request services from the kernel (e.g., opening a file, creating a process, sending network data).

quiz.question: What is the Windows Registry?
quiz.explanation: The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database storing configuration settings for the OS and applications. It is a common persistence mechanism for malware.

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT a valid Linux file system?
quiz.explanation: NTFS is a Windows file system. ext4, XFS, and Btrfs are all native Linux file systems.

quiz.question: What does UAC do?
quiz.explanation: User Account Control (UAC) prompts for administrator consent before allowing system changes. It helps prevent unauthorized modifications but can be bypassed by sophisticated malware.

quiz.question: What is a privileged escalation attack?
quiz.explanation: Privilege escalation is when an attacker gains higher-level access than they should have. Kernel exploits are a common vector for privilege escalation.

quiz.question: Which OS uses the XNU kernel?
quiz.explanation: macOS uses the XNU (X is Not Unix) kernel, which combines the Mach microkernel with BSD components.

quiz.question: What is AppArmor?
quiz.explanation: AppArmor is a Mandatory Access Control (MAC) framework for Linux that restricts program capabilities using profiles. It is an alternative to SELinux.

quiz.question: What is the order of volatility in forensics?
quiz.explanation: Order of volatility: capture CPU registers first (most volatile), then RAM, then storage. Network state is captured alongside RAM. This preserves the most volatile evidence first.

quiz.question: What is a rootkit?
options: Malware that hides in the kernel
quiz.explanation: A rootkit is malware that infects the kernel or boot process to hide its presence from security tools. It can intercept system calls to conceal files, processes, and network connections.

topics.title: Day 3: Binary, Hex, and Number Systems
topics.description: Master binary, hexadecimal, and data representation for security work
topics.content:
## Day 3: Binary, Hex, and Number Systems

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Hex fluency is a basic requirement for forensic analysis and exploit development. When a hiring manager asks you to read a memory dump or decode a packet capture, you need to recognize patterns in hex instantly. It separates script kiddies from real analysts.

:::objectives
- Master binary
- Hexadecimal
- Data representation for security work
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Generate a practice worksheet for converting between binary, hex, and decimal. Include 10 exercises with answers.
topics.interviewQuestion: Convert decimal 255 to binary and hex without a calculator.
topics.interviewAnswer: 255 = 11111111 binary (all 8 bits set) = 0xFF hex. Method: divide by 2 repeatedly for binary, divide by 16 for hex.

quiz.question: What is 0xFF in decimal?
quiz.explanation: 0xFF is hex for 255 decimal. F = 15, so 15*16 + 15 = 255.

quiz.question: How many bits per hex digit?
quiz.explanation: Each hex digit represents 4 bits (a nibble). Two hex digits = 1 byte.

quiz.question: Binary for decimal 10?
quiz.explanation: 10 in decimal = 1010 in binary (8 + 0 + 2 + 0).

quiz.question: Hex for binary 11001100?
File signature starting with 0x25504446?
quiz.explanation: 0x25504446 = '%PDF' in ASCII. PDF files start with this magic number.

quiz.question: How many values can 8 bits represent?
quiz.explanation: 8 bits = 2^8 = 256 possible values (0-255).

quiz.question: What is binary 00010001 in decimal?
What is 0x1A in decimal?
quiz.explanation: 0x1A = 1*16 + 10 = 26 in decimal. A = 10 in hex.

quiz.question: Which number system is base-16?
quiz.explanation: Hexadecimal is base-16, using digits 0-9 and letters A-F.

quiz.question: What is the hex value of decimal 31?
What is the subnet mask 255.255.255.0 in hex?
quiz.explanation: 255 decimal = 0xFF hex. So 255.255.255.0 = 0xFF.0xFF.0xFF.0x00.

quiz.question: What are magic bytes used for in forensics?
quiz.explanation: Magic bytes (file signatures) are the first few bytes of a file that identify its type. Forensic tools use them for file carving even when extensions are changed.

quiz.question: Binary 10000000 in decimal?
What is the AND result of 1100 and 1010?
quiz.explanation: 1100 AND 1010 = 1000. AND: both bits must be 1. Only the first bit position (8) has 1 in both.

quiz.question: What is the XOR result of 1100 and 1010?
quiz.explanation: 1100 XOR 1010 = 0110. XOR: bits differ = 1, bits same = 0. XOR is fundamental to encryption algorithms.

topics.title: Day 4: Introduction to Virtualization
topics.description: Learn about VMs, hypervisors, and why they matter in security
topics.content:
## Day 4: Introduction to Virtualization - Lab Exercise

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** Virtualization is how real security teams work. SOC analysts spin up VMs to investigate malware. Pentesters build isolated lab networks. Cloud security relies entirely on virtualized infrastructure. Your home lab is your ticket to building real skills safely.

:::objectives
- Learn about VMs
- Hypervisors
- Why they matter in security
:::
topics.aiPrompt: Explain Type 1 vs Type 2 hypervisors with real-world examples of each.
topics.interviewQuestion: What is the difference between Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors?
topics.interviewAnswer: Type 1 (bare-metal) runs directly on hardware, more efficient, used in data centers (VMware ESXi, Hyper-V). Type 2 (hosted) runs on top of an OS, easier for desktop use (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).

quiz.question: Type 1 hypervisor?
quiz.explanation: Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisors run directly on hardware. Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen.

quiz.question: Which network mode gives a VM its own IP?
quiz.explanation: Bridged mode connects the VM directly to the physical network, giving it its own IP address on the same subnet as the host.

quiz.question: What is VirtualBox?
quiz.explanation: VirtualBox is a Type 2 (hosted) hypervisor that runs on top of Windows, Linux, or macOS.

quiz.question: Why use Host-Only for malware analysis?
quiz.explanation: Host-Only networking isolates VMs from your physical network and the internet, preventing malware from spreading while still allowing VM-to-VM communication.

quiz.question: What is Metasploitable 2?
quiz.explanation: Metasploitable 2 is a deliberately vulnerable Linux VM designed for practicing penetration testing skills.

quiz.question: Minimum RAM recommended for a security home lab?
quiz.explanation: 8 GB is the minimum for running a host OS plus one VM. 16 GB is recommended for running multiple VMs simultaneously.

quiz.question: What is a VM snapshot?
quiz.explanation: A snapshot saves the exact state of a VM (memory, disk, settings). You can revert to a snapshot after making changes, which is essential for testing malware or exploits.

quiz.question: Which cloud model uses virtualization?
quiz.explanation: All cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) rely on virtualization to abstract physical hardware and provide on-demand resources.

quiz.question: What does VT-x enable?
quiz.explanation: VT-x (Intel) and AMD-V are CPU extensions that enable hardware-assisted virtualization, improving VM performance and security.

quiz.question: What is a container compared to a VM?
quiz.explanation: Containers share the host OS kernel while VMs each have their own kernel. Containers are lighter weight but less isolated.

quiz.question: What is VirtualBox Guest Additions?
quiz.explanation: Guest Additions installs drivers for better mouse integration, shared clipboard, resizable display, and shared folders between host and VM.

quiz.question: How does a VM access the physical network via NAT?
quiz.explanation: With NAT mode, the host acts as a router. The VM uses a private IP, and the host translates traffic to/from the physical network.

quiz.question: What is live migration in enterprise hypervisors?
options: Moving a running VM between hosts
quiz.explanation: Live migration moves a running VM from one physical host to another with zero downtime, enabling maintenance without service interruption.

quiz.question: What is a virtual switch?
options: Software-based network forwarding in a hypervisor
quiz.explanation: A virtual switch is software within the hypervisor that forwards network traffic between VMs and physical network interfaces.

quiz.question: What risk does VM sprawl introduce?
quiz.explanation: VM sprawl is the uncontrolled proliferation of VMs, leading to unmanaged, unpatched systems that increase attack surface.

topics.title: Day 5: Building Your Home Lab
topics.description: Set up a complete security lab environment
topics.content:
## Day 5: Building Your Home Lab - Project

> **Why This Gets You Hired:** A documented home lab is one of the strongest portfolio pieces you can show. Hiring managers love seeing initiative. A screenshot of Kali pinging Metasploitable proves you can set up a testing environment. List it on your resume under 'Projects' immediately.

:::objectives
- Set up a complete security lab environment
:::
topics.aiPrompt: List 10 essential VMs for a cybersecurity home lab and explain what each is used for.
topics.interviewQuestion: How do you set up a safe home lab for practicing pentesting?
topics.interviewAnswer: Use VirtualBox with Host-Only networking to isolate lab traffic. Essential VMs: Kali (attacker), Metasploitable 2 (target), Windows 10 (target), Ubuntu Server (Linux target), pfSense (firewall). Never connect vulnerable VMs to your home network.

quiz.question: Minimum RAM recommended for home lab?
quiz.explanation: 8 GB is the practical minimum for a host + one VM. 16 GB is recommended for running multiple VMs.

quiz.question: Which network mode isolates lab VMs?
quiz.explanation: Host-Only networking creates an isolated virtual network that VMs can communicate on but cannot reach the internet or physical network.

quiz.question: What is Kali Linux used for?
quiz.explanation: Kali Linux is a Debian-based distribution pre-loaded with 600+ security tools for penetration testing and security research.

quiz.question: Why a separate lab network?
quiz.explanation: A separate lab network prevents your vulnerable VMs from being accessible on your home network, avoiding legal liability if they are compromised and used to attack others.

quiz.question: Kali package manager?
quiz.explanation: Kali Linux uses apt (Advanced Package Tool), inherited from Debian. Commands: sudo apt update, sudo apt install <package>.

quiz.question: What is Metasploitable 2?
quiz.explanation: Metasploitable 2 is an intentionally vulnerable Linux VM created by Rapid7 for practicing security testing.

quiz.question: What is the first command to run after installing Kali?
options: sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade
quiz.explanation: Always update your package lists and upgrade installed packages first to ensure you have the latest tools and security patches.

quiz.question: What is a snapshot used for in a lab?
quiz.explanation: Snapshots save the complete state of a VM. Take a snapshot before running exploits so you can revert to a clean state and try again.

quiz.question: Which VM would you use as a Linux attack target?
quiz.explanation: Metasploitable 2 is the most common Linux target VM for practicing penetration testing at the beginner level.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of a lab README?
quiz.explanation: A lab README documents your setup, configurations, findings, and lessons learned. It serves as a portfolio artifact that employers can review.

quiz.question: Why should vulnerable VMs never use Bridged networking?
options: They would be accessible on your home network
They would not get IP addresses
quiz.explanation: Bridged networking exposes the VM directly on your home network, making it accessible to other devices and potentially to attackers on the internet.

quiz.question: What additional security tool can you add to your lab?
quiz.explanation: Adding a pfSense VM as a firewall between your Kali and target VMs gives you practice with network segmentation and traffic monitoring.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of keeping lab VMs off the internet?
options: Prevent accidental attacks on real systems
quiz.explanation: Lab VMs, especially intentionally vulnerable ones, should be isolated from the internet to prevent them from being compromised and used in real attacks.

quiz.question: How do you transfer files between host and VM?
quiz.explanation: VirtualBox shared folders or SCP (if SSH is enabled) are the recommended ways to transfer files between host and VM safely.

quiz.question: What is the recommended next step after building your home lab?
quiz.explanation: After building your lab, practice network scanning with nmap between Kali and Metasploitable. This is where real learning begins.
